summary: Gruber publishes his annual Apple report card for 2023, giving the Mac and iPhone top marks (A) while expressing frustration with the iPad's stagnant lineup (C) and developer relations (C). He highlights the M3 MacBook Pros as the best ever, praises the iPhone 15 Pro's titanium design and weight reduction, and notes the Mac Pro as the sole weak spot in an otherwise excellent Mac lineup. The Apple Watch, Apple TV, and Services all earn solid B grades, with particular praise for WatchOS 10's redesign and AirPods Pro 2. He repeats longstanding criticisms about iCloud's miserly 5GB free tier and Apple's deteriorating relationship with developers over App Store policies.
key_points: Every Mac in the lineup except the Mac Pro is arguably in the best shape that model has ever been, with the M3 MacBook Pros leading the pack
The iPad earns a 'gentleman's C' due to no new hardware, a confusing lineup, and iPadOS remaining a no-man's land between Mac capability and iPhone portability
Developer relations gets a C for the third consecutive year, with App Store resentment building and Apple failing to make developers want to create software exclusively for its platforms
Apple's 5GB free iCloud tier remains inexcusably miserly, leaving countless iPhone users without device backups
WatchOS 10 represents the biggest rethink of the Watch software platform ever, with widgets proving more useful than apps
money_quotes: By the end of the year, every single Mac in the lineup, save one, is arguably in the best shape that model has ever been.
iPadOS remains fine, but to me still seems like the no-man's land platform: nowhere near as capable productivity-wise as a Mac; nowhere near as portable as an iPhone.
Apple's goal should be for developer relations to be so good that developers want to create software exclusively for Apple's platforms. The opposite is happening.
I wonder how many zillions of iPhone users out there don't have device backups because they only have a free iCloud account with 5 GB?
Big picture, this whole thing still feels like it's always poised to get good 'next year'. 2023 wasn't that year (again).
I have concerns and complaints about aspects of the direction Apple's software design is headed, but their software reliability has been very good for me.
I think widgets are generally more useful on Apple Watch than apps are.
The weight reduction is dramatic, and the titanium feels so much nicer in hand.
key_insight: Apple's hardware across Mac and iPhone reached historic highs in 2023, but the company's recurring failures in developer relations, iCloud generosity, and iPad strategy reveal persistent blind spots that strong hardware alone cannot fix.
